questiontext.text:
The transfer function of an ideal PID controller is:

$$C(s) = K_p\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$$

Which term provides integral action (eliminates steady-state error)?

generalfeedback.text: The $\frac{K_p}{T_i s}$ term is the integrator — it accumulates error over time and drives steady-state error to zero.

answer.text: Kₚ / (Tᵢ s) — the 1/(Tᵢ s) term

feedback.text: ✓ Correct. The integral term accumulates error over time, eliminating offset.

answer.text: Kₚ — the proportional gain

feedback.text: Proportional action reduces error but cannot eliminate steady-state offset on its own.

answer.text: Kₚ Tᴅ s — the derivative term

feedback.text: The derivative term reacts to the rate of change of error; it does not eliminate steady-state error.

answer.text: All three terms together

feedback.text: Only the integral term specifically eliminates steady-state error.

questiontext.text: In a PID controller, increasing the proportional gain $K_p$ will:

generalfeedback.text: Higher $K_p$ speeds up the response and reduces (but does not eliminate) steady-state error, but it also increases overshoot and can cause instability.

answer.text: Speed up the response and increase overshoot, potentially causing instability

feedback.text: ✓ Correct. Higher Kp gives a faster but more oscillatory (or even unstable) response.

answer.text: Eliminate steady-state error completely

feedback.text: Integral action is needed to eliminate steady-state error; Kp alone only reduces it.

answer.text: Slow down the response and reduce oscillation

feedback.text: Increasing Kp speeds up the response — derivative action helps reduce oscillation.

answer.text: Have no effect on system stability

feedback.text: Very high Kp can destabilise a system through excessive overshoot and oscillation.

questiontext.text: True or False: Applying the derivative term to the measurement (rather than the error) avoids the "derivative kick" that occurs on step setpoint changes.

generalfeedback.text: When the setpoint steps, the error jumps instantly, giving an infinite derivative. Differentiating the process variable (measurement) instead avoids this spike.

answer.text: true

feedback.text: ✓ Correct. This technique is called "derivative on measurement" and is standard in industrial PID.

answer.text: false

feedback.text: Incorrect — differentiating the measurement is a well-known fix for derivative kick.

answer.text: Setting Ti → ∞ and Td → 0, then increasing Kp until sustained oscillations occur

feedback.text: ✓ Correct. The critical gain Ku and period Tu are measured, then used to set PID parameters.

answer.text: Minimising the integral of the squared error (ISE) numerically

feedback.text: ISE minimisation is an optimisation-based method, not Ziegler–Nichols.

answer.text: Fitting a first-order plus dead-time (FOPDT) model to a step response

feedback.text: That is the Ziegler–Nichols step-response method, a different variant.

answer.text: Using a Bode plot to find the gain and phase margins

feedback.text: Gain/phase margin tuning uses frequency-domain analysis, not the Z–N ultimate-gain procedure.

questiontext.text: The gain margin of a control system is defined as:

generalfeedback.text: At the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ where $\angle L(j\omega) = -180°$, the gain margin is $GM = 1/|L(j\omega_{pc})|$. In dB: $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}|L(j\omega_{pc})|$.

answer.text: The factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased before the closed-loop becomes unstable

feedback.text: ✓ Correct. GM = 1/|L(jω)| evaluated at the -180° phase crossing frequency.

answer.text: The additional phase lag that would make the system marginally stable

feedback.text: That is the phase margin, not the gain margin.

answer.text: The maximum steady-state error for a unit step input

feedback.text: Steady-state error is related to the position error constant, not gain margin.

answer.text: The frequency at which the open-loop gain equals 0 dB

feedback.text: That is the gain crossover frequency — used to define phase margin, not gain margin.

questiontext.text:
An open-loop transfer function has magnitude $|L(j\omega_{pc})| = 0.25$ at the phase crossover frequency. What is the gain margin in dB?

Hint: $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}|L(j\omega_{pc})|$
Match each PID term to its primary effect on closed-loop behaviour.

answer.text: Reduces error magnitude; speeds up response
Eliminates steady-state error
Damps oscillations; improves transient response
